title: Sfogliatelle
subtitle: Neapolitan layered pastry filled with ricotta and semolina
excerpt: The Neapolitan pastry of many shelled, flaky layers enclosing a fragrant filling of ricotta, semolina, cinnamon, and orange zest. Making the layered dough at home is demanding but entirely possible; the result is the flakiest, crispest pastry most people will ever encounter.
sourceNotes: Sfogliatelle originate in the Convent of Santa Rosa in the Amalfi Coast region, where nuns are documented as having made a layered pastry in the seventeenth century. The recipe was adapted by a Neapolitan pastry shop in the early nineteenth century and became the iconic pastry of Naples. The sfogliatelle ricci (the flaky shell-shaped variety, as opposed to the frolla short-pastry version) is documented in Italian culinary literature and is public domain.

content.text: Sfogliatelle ricci are made from a stiff dough stretched paper-thin, spread with lard, and rolled tightly into a log. The log is sliced into rounds, and each round is opened by pressing from the centre outward to form a fluted shell. The

of fat between the dough layers produces the hundreds of flaky, shattering strata that make sfogliatelle unmistakeable in texture. The filling, a cooked mixture of ricotta or fresh curd, semolina, and orange zest, sets inside the shell as the pastry bakes.

The dough must be stretched as thin as possible. A pasta machine helps enormously on the final passes; without one, work the dough in small sections with a rolling pin, stretching with the backs of your hands as you would for strudel. The dough is forgiving of tears, which will be invisible in the finished pastry.

attrs.prepNote: or ricotta, well drained, for the filling

content.text: Combine {{plain-flour}} (dough quantity), {{semolina}} (dough quantity), and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Add 150 ml of cold water and mix to a very stiff dough, stiffer than bread dough. Knead for 10 minutes until smooth. Rest for 30 minutes covered. Work with one-quarter of the dough at a time; keep the rest covered. Roll or stretch each piece as thin as possible, ideally 1 to 2 mm, approaching translucent. Spread a thin layer of {{lard}} over the surface, then roll up tightly from one end into a cylinder. Stack the cylinders end to end and roll the whole log very gently to consolidate. Wrap in cling film and refrigerate for 2 hours.

Bring {{whole-milk}} to a gentle simmer in a saucepan. Whisk in the filling-quantity {{semolina}} and {{caster-sugar}} and stir constantly over medium heat for 4 to 5 minutes until thick. Remove from the heat and cool to room temperature. Beat in {{cream-cheese}}, {{eggs}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{vanilla-extract}}, and the zest from {{orange}}. Refrigerate until needed.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan (220°C conventional) and line baking trays with baking paper. Slice the chilled log into rounds about 2 cm thick. For each round: hold it between your thumbs and forefingers and press the centre gently outward to open the layered coil into a cone or shell shape about 8 cm across. Fill with 1 to 2 tablespoons of filling; press the edges together firmly to seal. Place on the prepared trays. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until deeply golden and the layers have separated and crisped. The base should be firm and dry, not soft. Cool for 10 minutes, then dust with {{icing-sugar}}. Eat warm.
Sfogliatelle are best eaten hot from the oven, within an hour of baking. The layers soften as they cool and the crisp texture is lost.
A pasta machine simplifies the dough-stretching significantly. Pass the dough down to the thinnest setting (or one above) before spreading with lard.
Sfogliatelle frolla is the easier sibling: the same filling inside a shortcrust pastry shell. Less dramatic in texture but far simpler to make.
